roots.title: Neural Networks Overview and Course Evaluations
roots.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, clarifying that this topic will not be included in the final exam, labs, or homework. A demonstration is provided by the lecturer, followed by a demo by Joy. Logistical details include a link to the presentation and instructions for joining Slido for interaction. Course evaluations, both internal and official, will contribute 25% towards final course credit, with a possibility of 0.5% extra credit for achieving 90% participation. The internal survey aims to gather feedback for course improvement, while the introduction to neural networks is designed to foster confidence for independent learning in deep learning. The discussion explores the complexity of the brain and its impact on neural network development, noting that the brain comprises approximately 10 billion neurons communicating rapidly through spikes of information, performing hundreds of operations per second, similar to neural network simulations. The structure of individual neurons is analyzed, focusing on dendrites that receive signals through chemical reactions. Neurons process information through their structure, with the cell body deciding activation and the axon transmitting signals. Artificial neurons replicate this by processing inputs through dendrites, with the cell body computing a weighted sum to produce an output, akin to linear regression. The segment details the role of weights in this computation and describes the structure of artificial neurons, including a summation unit and an activation function, which are crucial in binary classification.

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Course Logistics
children.summary: The lecture introduces the topic of neural networks, emphasizing that it will not be covered in the final exam, labs, or homework. The session includes a demonstration by the lecturer and a follow-up demo by Joy. Additionally, logistical details are provided, including a link to the presentation and information about joining Slido for interaction.

children.title: Course Evaluations and Introduction to Neural Networks
children.summary: Completion of both the internal and official campus course evaluations will contribute 25% towards final course credit, with an additional 0.5% extra credit for classes with 90% participation. The internal survey seeks feedback for course improvement. The introduction to neural networks aims to build confidence for independent learning in deep learning, alongside discussing the final course survey for enhancing future offerings.

children.title: Course Evaluation and Introduction to Neural Networks
children.summary: Completion of both the internal course evaluation and the official campus course evaluation will contribute 25% towards final course credit. If 90% of the class participates in both evaluations, those who complete them will receive an additional 0.5% extra credit. The internal survey aims to gather feedback for improving the course. The lecture introduces neural networks, aiming to build confidence for further independent learning in deep learning, and discusses the final course survey designed to improve future course offerings.

children.title: Course Evaluation and Extra Credit Details
children.summary: Completion of both the internal course evaluation and the official campus course evaluation will contribute 25% towards final course credit. If 90% of the class participates in both evaluations, those who complete them will receive an additional 0.5% extra credit. The internal survey aims to gather feedback for improving the course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I talked about it last time about course evaluation. If you feel other internal course evaluation and the campus course evaluation, you're going to get 25% towards your final course credit. If 90% of the class fills out both of these evaluation forms, then those who fill out the form is going to get an additional 0.5%. So it's a great deal. Take advantage of it. We really want to get feedback in terms of how whether the parts in data 100 that are going well, whether the parts that needs a little bit more attention, because they feel like that the course again is a stable, but whether the things that you're observing that we can improve on. children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Course Evaluation
children.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, aiming to build confidence for further independent learning in the area of deep learning. Additionally, it discusses the final course survey, which is designed to improve future course offerings. Students who complete both the internal anonymous survey and the official Course Evaluation will receive 0.5% extra credit, with a potential additional 0.5% for the entire class if participation reaches 90%.

children.title: Neural Networks and Brain Structure Overview
children.summary: The discussion explores the complexity of the brain and its impact on neural network development, drawing insights from cognitive science and neuroscience. It notes that the brain comprises approximately 10 billion neurons that communicate rapidly through spikes of information and perform hundreds of operations per second, akin to neural network simulations. The structure of individual neurons is also analyzed, with a particular focus on dendrites, which receive signals through chemical reactions between neurons.

children.title: Neural Networks and Brain Structure Overview
children.summary: The discussion covers the complexity of the brain and its influence on neural network development, emphasizing insights from cognitive science and neuroscience. It highlights that the brain consists of approximately 10 billion neurons communicating rapidly through spikes of information, performing hundreds of operations per second, similar to neural network simulations. The structure of individual neurons is also examined, particularly focusing on dendrites, which receive signals through chemical reactions between neurons.

children.title: Neural Networks and Brain Structure Overview
children.summary: The discussion covers the complexity of the brain and its influence on neural network development, emphasizing insights from cognitive science and neuroscience. It notes that the brain consists of approximately 10 billion neurons that communicate rapidly through spikes of information, performing hundreds of operations per second, akin to neural network simulations. Additionally, the structure of individual neurons is examined, particularly the dendrites, which receive signals via chemical reactions between neurons.

children.title: Neural Networks Inspired by Brain Structure
children.summary: The discussion begins with an overview of the brain's complexity and the rudimentary understanding of its functioning. It highlights the inspiration drawn from cognitive science and neuroscience in the development of neural networks. The brain contains approximately 10 billion neurons, which communicate rapidly through spikes of information. This communication involves hundreds of operations per second, paralleling the simulation of neural networks. The lecture also touches on the structure of individual neurons, specifically the dendrites, which are branches that receive signals through chemical reactions between neurons.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm going to start by talking a little bit about Some kind of a primer into how we can think about neural networks. I'm going to talk about brain first. So what is the structure of the brain we're not going to go into the details of how the brain works, but just realizing that brain is a very complex phenomenon and then the understanding of how brain works and how each individual neuron actually works is very kind of our understanding is very rudimentary and then. what we're trying to get inspiration from in building neural networks is different concepts in brain. And there is a lot of collaboration between cognitive science, neuroscience, and also getting inspiration from those as people started building neural networks in different deep architectures that are now kind of very famous in the field. So a neuron, like brain has 10 billions of neurons, The switching time between the neurons is very fast. Each neuron tries to fire some kind of information, and then the other neurons is going to receive those spikes. And then these spikes are going to be propagated through the entire network of the brain and all of the neurons in the brain. There are generally hundreds of operations being done per second. So that's how you can also think about how we want to make that the way that we're simulating neural networks is kind of working in the same way. But thinking about one individual neuron and different parts of an individual, like biological neuron, that we are trying to simulate in an artificial neuron, is kind of different pieces. So the different pieces of a neuron, there's like, den writes that neurons have, and these den writes are actually different branches that reach signal. signal. So you're thinking about then rice being here. So it is kind of spread that branch like it receives the signals through different chemical reactions that that appears between multiple neurons that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: So I'm going to start by talking a little bit about Some kind of a primer into how we can think about neural networks. I'm going to talk about brain first. So what is the structure of the brain we're not going to go into the details of how the brain works, but just realizing that brain is a very complex phenomenon and then the understanding of how brain works and how each individual neuron actually works is very kind of our understanding is very rudimentary and then. what we're trying to get inspiration from in building neural networks is different concepts in brain. And there is a lot of collaboration between cognitive science, neuroscience, and also getting inspiration from those as people started building neural networks in different deep architectures that are now kind of very famous in the field. So a neuron, like brain has 10 billions of neurons, The switching time between the neurons is very fast. Each neuron tries to fire some kind of information, and then the other neurons is going to receive those spikes. And then these spikes are going to be propagated through the entire network of the brain and all of the neurons in the brain. There are generally hundreds of operations being done per second. So that's how you can also think about how we want to make that the way that we're simulating neural networks is kind of working in the same way. But thinking about one individual neuron and different parts of an individual, like biological neuron, that we are trying to simulate in an artificial neuron, is kind of different pieces. So the different pieces of a neuron, there's like, den writes that neurons have, and these den writes are actually different branches that reach signal. signal. So you're thinking about then rice being here. So it is kind of spread that branch like it receives the signals through different chemical reactions that that appears between multiple neurons that we have.

children.title: Neurons, Artificial Neurons, and Activation Functions
children.summary: Neurons process information through their structure, where the cell body decides activation and the axon transmits signals via electro-chemical processes. Artificial neurons replicate this by processing inputs through dendrites, with the cell body computing a weighted sum to produce an output, akin to linear regression. The segment details the role of weights in this computation and describes the structure of artificial neurons, including a summation unit and an activation function. Activation functions are crucial in binary classification, with the step function establishing linear decision boundaries for linearly separable data, though it faces limitations due to non-differentiability. The sigmoid function serves as a differentiable alternative, mapping inputs to probabilities for classification. The discussion emphasizes the necessity of activation functions in neurons and neural networks, comparing neurons to logical gates and concluding with a simulation of an AND gate.

children.title: Neurons and Artificial Neurons: Structure and Function
children.summary: The cell body of a neuron processes information to decide activation, while the axon transmits signals to synapses, which can be excitatory or inhibitory. This involves electro-chemical signals traveling from dendrites through the cell body and along the axon. Artificial neurons mimic biological neurons by processing inputs through dendrites, with the cell body computing a weighted combination of these inputs to produce an output. This is compared to linear regression, where inputs are multiplied by weights to form a linear function leading to activation. The segment includes a schematic representation of inputs and a bias term, detailing how the summation unit calculates the weighted sum of inputs, emphasizing the role of weights, and concludes with the structure of an artificial neuron comprising a summation unit and an activation function.

children.title: Structure and Function of Neurons
children.summary: The cell body of a neuron processes and aggregates information to decide on activation. The axon transmits signals to synapses, which connect neurons and can be classified as excitatory, stimulating the next neuron to fire, or inhibitory, indicating insufficient signals for activation. This process involves electro-chemical signals traveling from dendrites through the cell body and along the axon.

children.title: Structure and Function of Neurons
children.summary: The cell body of a neuron acts as a processing unit that collects and aggregates information to determine if the neuron will activate. The axon transmits signals to synapses, which are connections between neurons. Synapses can be classified as excitatory, which stimulate the next neuron to fire, or inhibitory, which indicate insufficient signals to activate the next neuron. This process involves electro-chemical signals propagating from dendrites through the cell body and down the axon.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There is so more cell body which is kind of a unit that tries to collect information and aggregate information and decide whether the neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So you can think about it as kind of a processing unit in a biological neuron that we have. Once the process is actually done and then axon is going to transmit all of those signals to the synopsis and these synopsis are the connections that we're making or the neuron is making two other neurons to pass information along. So you can think about these synopsis as different parts in different connections between different neurons that that either is going to send a chemical reaction to activate the next neuron or not activate the next neuron. So, we are calling these synopsis as excitatory synopsis or inhibitory synopsis. Excitedory means that the next neuron is going to fire. It has received indication that some kind of stimulation has happened in the previous neuron or inhibitory means that there is lack of enough information or signal in the previous neurons that there's no enough signal or chemical reactions that can be passed through the entire network. leaf_chunk.audio: There is so more cell body which is kind of a unit that tries to collect information and aggregate information and decide whether the neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So you can think about it as kind of a processing unit in a biological neuron that we have. Once the process is actually done and then axon is going to transmit all of those signals to the synopsis and these synopsis are the connections that we're making or the neuron is making two other neurons to pass information along. So you can think about these synopsis as different parts in different connections between different neurons that that either is going to send a chemical reaction to activate the next neuron or not activate the next neuron. So, we are calling these synopsis as excitatory synopsis or inhibitory synopsis. Excitedory means that the next neuron is going to fire. It has received indication that some kind of stimulation has happened in the previous neuron or inhibitory means that there is lack of enough information or signal in the previous neurons that there's no enough signal or chemical reactions that can be passed through the entire network.

children.title: Artificial Neurons: Structure and Function
children.summary: Artificial neurons are designed to mimic biological neurons, processing inputs through dendrites that collect signals. The cell body computes a weighted combination of these inputs to produce an output using a function. The segment compares artificial neurons to linear regression, explaining that inputs are multiplied by weights to form a linear function, resulting in activation. It details how neurons activate based on weighted inputs, with a schematic representation showing inputs labeled x1 through xd and a bias term. The summation unit calculates the weighted sum of inputs, reflecting the importance of each weight. Finally, the artificial neuron comprises a summation unit and an activation function, which processes the weighted sum to determine the output.

children.title: Artificial Neurons and Biological Neuron Structure
children.summary: Artificial neurons mimic biological neurons by processing inputs received through dendrites, which collect signals from other neurons. The cell body calculates a weighted combination of these inputs to determine the output, applying a function to this combination. The discussion highlights the similarities between biological neuron structures, including dendrites for input collection, the soma for processing, and synapses for information exchange, which can be either excitatory or inhibitory.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what we're actually trying to mimic in an artificial neuron is exactly the same thing. So we're trying to read inputs through the end rights. We have a processing unit and then we're trying to actually transmit those information that be processed into the next neurons. And we want to make sure that as we process the information, as we have some kind of activation or information that we're trying to share with other neurons, those activations are also So kind of working in the same way, they're excited or in inhibitory meaning that the next neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So what we're trying to do, again, artificial neuron has a similar kind of reaction. So remember that we had dendrites, so these dendrites as inputs. So you're thinking about these dendrites inputs receiving information through the synaptic connections to the previous neurons. So here we also have the same thing. So we have inputs going into the neuron that we have. And then the neuron actually is going to use it similar cell body in order to calculate a weighted combination of those inputs or those reactions that it has received in order to decide what the output is going to be. And the output is going to be a function that is applied to a weighted combination of inputs that it has received. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So what we're actually trying to mimic in an artificial neuron is exactly the same thing. So we're trying to read inputs through the end rights. We have a processing unit and then we're trying to actually transmit those information that be processed into the next neurons. And we want to make sure that as we process the information, as we have some kind of activation or information that we're trying to share with other neurons, those activations are also So kind of working in the same way, they're excited or in inhibitory meaning that the next neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So what we're trying to do, again, artificial neuron has a similar kind of reaction. So remember that we had dendrites, so these dendrites as inputs. So you're thinking about these dendrites inputs receiving information through the synaptic connections to the previous neurons. So here we also have the same thing. So we have inputs going into the neuron that we have. And then the neuron actually is going to use it similar cell body in order to calculate a weighted combination of those inputs or those reactions that it has received in order to decide what the output is going to be. And the output is going to be a function that is applied to a weighted combination of inputs that it has received.

children.title: Artificial Neurons and Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of an artificial neuron by comparing it to simple linear regression. It explains that inputs are multiplied by different weights and combined to form a linear function, which produces a value referred to as activation. The discussion emphasizes that this linear combination reflects the degree of activation of the neuron.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So these equations you've seen them before. So we're going to formally define what function F is going to look like whether those parameters, whether those inputs, and so on and so forth in the first part of today's lecture. So let's go through the very first and the simplest way that we can think about the neuron. We can think about the neuron as just the artificial neuron as just a simple linear regression. It has inputs going into the neuron, right? And the simplest thing that we learned was linear regression. So what do we do? We have all of those inputs and then I would say that, okay, I'm going to get those inputs. I'm going to multiply these inputs with different weights. I'm going to combine them. This is a linear function and this linear function is going to give me value that for the purpose of this lecture I'm just going to call this value as an activation. children.title: Weighted Inputs and Activation in Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses how neurons activate based on a weighted combination of inputs received from other neurons. It explains the schematic representation of a biological neuron, detailing inputs labeled x1 through xd, along with a bias term. Each input is associated with a weight, with the bias term represented by theta zero. The process involves calculating a weighted sum of these inputs, where each weight corresponds to a parameter that will be learned. This summation unit reflects how the neuron values incoming information differently based on the assigned weights.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is kind of an indication that looking at the weighted combination of those inputs that we have received either through the input or through the connections from different neurons are we going to activate this neuron or not activate this neuron. And this why has value right now is our activation for that purpose. So a very simple kind of structure. If you actually think about it in terms of like, schematically, how is going to relate to kind of a biological neuron? We have all of those inputs. So notice that I have x1 through xd. And then I also have one indication for the biosterm. Remember the biostrict that we had. So we would augment the feature space that we have one column of one values. And then that was the input that would correspond to the bias term or the theta zero that we have. So what we're going to do is we're going to weight all of these inputs. So the weight associated to the bias term is going to be just the bias parameter theta zero. The weight associated to x1 is going to be theta one and all the way to the last feature is going to be theta d. So we're going to multiply them. So calculating a weighted kind of combination of all of these ones and then we're summing up all of these way, the multiplications that we calculated through the inputs and the parameters that we have. So that's a summation unit. Remember that I said, so more cell body is a summation and activation unit. So this is a summation piece that we're going to value the information that we're receiving from different inputs differently through the weights that we received. So the weight of tet A1, tet A2D and tet A0, they're all going to be different values that we're going learn and what we're going to do is we're calculating this weighted combination. leaf_chunk.audio: So this is kind of an indication that looking at the weighted combination of those inputs that we have received either through the input or through the connections from different neurons are we going to activate this neuron or not activate this neuron. And this why has value right now is our activation for that purpose. So a very simple kind of structure. If you actually think about it in terms of like, schematically, how is going to relate to kind of a biological neuron? We have all of those inputs. So notice that I have x1 through xd. And then I also have one indication for the biosterm. Remember the biostrict that we had. So we would augment the feature space that we have one column of one values. And then that was the input that would correspond to the bias term or the theta zero that we have. So what we're going to do is we're going to weight all of these inputs. So the weight associated to the bias term is going to be just the bias parameter theta zero. The weight associated to x1 is going to be theta one and all the way to the last feature is going to be theta d. So we're going to multiply them. So calculating a weighted kind of combination of all of these ones and then we're summing up all of these way, the multiplications that we calculated through the inputs and the parameters that we have. So that's a summation unit. Remember that I said, so more cell body is a summation and activation unit. So this is a summation piece that we're going to value the information that we're receiving from different inputs differently through the weights that we received. So the weight of tet A1, tet A2D and tet A0, they're all going to be different values that we're going learn and what we're going to do is we're calculating this weighted combination.

children.title: Artificial Neuron: Summation and Activation Functions
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit calculates a weighted combination of inputs, which is then processed by the activation function to determine the neuron's output. This function can apply thresholding or other transformations to control the output, ensuring that not all inputs have direct control over the output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: our activation and output. So very simple artificial neuron. But again, remember that I said that a similar cell body is going to actually have summation unit plus also some activation function. So what does that activation function look like? I don't want to directly say that, okay, I'm calculating a weighted combination of inputs and there you go. This is your activation. I want to actually do some thresholding or some apply some kind of function so that I can control what the output is going to be. going to be. I don't want to give the direct control to all of those inputs that we have. They do have direct control, but I also want to add some kind of a linear or non-linear function as an activation function. So the way that that looks like is that, again, we're calculating the weighted combination of inputs that we have, right? children.title: Neural Activation Function Overview
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit calculates the weighted combination of inputs, which is then passed to the activation function to determine the neuron's activation. The activation function controls the output range of the neuron, typically limiting it to values between 0 and 1 or between -1 and 1. The negative output indicates inhibition of the neuron's firing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So again, what that looks like, schematically in this graph is that I had this summation unit. I will calculate the output. I'll pass the output to the activation function. The activation function is now going to output a value that again, the whole idea of this activation function is that we can control what value we put. And what is the range of the values that the neuron can generate so that it cannot generate. values from negative infinity to positive infinity. So we want to make sure that the output range is always controlled. So we could probably say that the output range is going to be between 0 and 1. Or the output range of dysfunction is going to be between negative 1 and 1. Negative 1, meaning that I'm trying to inhibit the neuron from firing. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So again, what that looks like, schematically in this graph is that I had this summation unit. I will calculate the output. I'll pass the output to the activation function. The activation function is now going to output a value that again, the whole idea of this activation function is that we can control what value we put. And what is the range of the values that the neuron can generate so that it cannot generate. values from negative infinity to positive infinity. So we want to make sure that the output range is always controlled. So we could probably say that the output range is going to be between 0 and 1. Or the output range of dysfunction is going to be between negative 1 and 1. Negative 1, meaning that I'm trying to inhibit the neuron from firing.

children.title: Artificial Neuron and Activation Function
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit calculates the weighted combination of inputs, and the resulting weighted summation is passed to the activation function to determine the neuron's activation. The output, represented as y-hat, is derived from applying the activation function to the summation value, illustrating the core concept of how neurons operate in neural networks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Positive 1 means that I'm trying to excite the output so that the next neuron is actually going to receive a positive value and then decide whether it wants to fire or not fire, based on this feature that it received. So that's the whole purpose of having activation function. It puts a reasonable output range for a specific neuron. And then now my output is actually going to be the result of applying. This activation function to the summation value or this z-input activation that I had, and then I have y-hat value. So this whole thing is just the entire idea of neuron. So you know all the different pieces together. You have learned different kinds of ways that we're trying to build different linear models. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: Positive 1 means that I'm trying to excite the output so that the next neuron is actually going to receive a positive value and then decide whether it wants to fire or not fire, based on this feature that it received. So that's the whole purpose of having activation function. It puts a reasonable output range for a specific neuron. And then now my output is actually going to be the result of applying. This activation function to the summation value or this z-input activation that I had, and then I have y-hat value. So this whole thing is just the entire idea of neuron. So you know all the different pieces together. You have learned different kinds of ways that we're trying to build different linear models.

children.title: Activation Functions in Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the optimization of feature selection and the role of activation functions in neural networks. An activation function can be a simple identity function where the output equals the input. Various types of activation functions will be explored, starting with the concept of a step function, which outputs either one or zero based on whether the input exceeds a certain threshold. The segment emphasizes understanding these functions as fundamental components of artificial neurons, which combine inputs through a weighted summation before applying the activation function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We optimize how we can actually come up with different features, do fence the different linear models. We're going to talk a little bit about what does function, the activation function f is going to look like. So what kind of activation functions we can have. One simple way is to just say that the activation function is going to be y equal to x input in, output out. So the same input output, one to one correspondence, but we're going to talk about different kinds of activation functions that we have. referring to different concepts in neural network, but if you dig deep and then have the patience to kind of learn about all different elements, it's just very not complicated. So the opposite of the things that people would think. So now let's talk about the first activation. Remember just a notion of being active, being not active, having the output being equal to one or output being equal to zero. What if I decide my activation function to be just a simple step function? Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Step Function Activation in Neurons
children.summary: A step function is defined for neuron activation based on input activation values. If the weighted combination of inputs is greater than or equal to zero, the neuron is activated, resulting in an output of 1. Conversely, if the input is negative, the neuron is not activated, and the output is 0.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The way that I'm going to define this a step function is simple, that I would say that if the input activation is greater than or equal to zero, I'm going to say that the neuron is activated. If the input, the weighted combination of inputs, or the input activation is a negative value, that means that there is lack of information from the inputs for me to say that the neuron is activated. children.title: Neuron Activation with Step Function
children.summary: The output of a neuron using a step function as its activation function is determined by the weighted combination of inputs. If this combination is negative, the output is 0, indicating the neuron is not activated. Conversely, if the combination is positive, the output is 1, indicating activation. The function can be expressed as f(z) = 1 if z ≥ 0 and f(z) = 0 if z < 0. The discussion assumes a two-dimensional dataset for simplicity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Therefore, I would say that the output is equal to zero, right? A simple step function. So what's happening here is that when you're on calculating the weighted combination of inputs and that's negative, I would say that this is you activated again the other way is going to be activating a neuron. So what's happening here is that again I do the summation as usual and then I'll pass it through activation function right now my activation function is a step function simple step function with the threshold at zero and then I'll get the output production. So what's happening after this is that if I have a data set and for simplicity, I'm assuming that my data set is a two dimensional data set. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Binary Classification and Decision Boundary
children.summary: The segment discusses binary classification, explaining how to determine whether data points belong to class zero or class one. It describes calculating the input activation using a weighted combination of inputs, represented by the dot product of the theta vector and the x vector, resulting in the input activation z. The decision boundary is defined where z equals zero, with points above this line classified as class one and those below as class zero. The application of a step function is also mentioned, reinforcing the classification criteria based on the position relative to the decision boundary.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And the task that I'm going to run is a binary classification. I want to say whether the data points belong to class zero or class one. So one thing that I can do is I can actually use exactly the same method that I had, right? I'm going to say that I'm calculating the activation or the input activation. I'm going to calculate the weighted combination of inputs or the dot product, which means the tetah vector and the x vector that's going to give me the input activation z. And I would say that this right here is my decision boundary, where z is equal to zero. And if I apply my step function here, I would say that any point that is above on or above this line is going to be like the class one and anything below this line is going to be is going to be like the class zero. Again, this is a line of z equal to zero. Anything above the line is going to be classified as class one. Anything below it is going to be classified as class zero. leaf_chunk.audio: And the task that I'm going to run is a binary classification. I want to say whether the data points belong to class zero or class one. So one thing that I can do is I can actually use exactly the same method that I had, right? I'm going to say that I'm calculating the activation or the input activation. I'm going to calculate the weighted combination of inputs or the dot product, which means the tetah vector and the x vector that's going to give me the input activation z. And I would say that this right here is my decision boundary, where z is equal to zero. And if I apply my step function here, I would say that any point that is above on or above this line is going to be like the class one and anything below this line is going to be is going to be like the class zero. Again, this is a line of z equal to zero. Anything above the line is going to be classified as class one. Anything below it is going to be classified as class zero.

children.title: Activation Functions in Binary Classification and Neural Networks
children.summary: Activation functions are essential in binary classification, transforming input activations into classifications and determining neuron activation. The step function is introduced for making classification decisions based on input Z, establishing a linear decision boundary for linearly separable data. The relationship between the step function and logistic regression is highlighted, noting their reliance on a linear component. The limitations of the step function are discussed, particularly its non-differentiability, which complicates gradient descent optimization. The sigmoid function is presented as a differentiable alternative, mapping inputs to a probabilistic range, with a threshold of 0.5 for classification. The importance of activation functions in neurons for binary classification is emphasized, along with the need for neural networks to overcome the limitations of individual neurons. An analogy is drawn between neurons and logical gates, and the segment concludes with simulating an AND gate using a neuron model.

children.title: Activation Functions and Binary Classification
children.summary: Activation functions are crucial in binary classification for transforming input activations into classifications, determining neuron activation and class membership (class 0 or class 1). The process involves input Z for classification decisions, illustrated by a step function that outputs based on whether Z is greater than or equal to zero. The step function establishes a linear decision boundary, enabling perfect classification of linearly separable data. The relationship between the step function and logistic regression is discussed, noting their shared reliance on a linear component (z) and the application of an activation function to derive probabilities. A question is raised regarding the absence of the step function in prior discussions on logistic regression.

children.title: Activation Functions in Binary Classification
children.summary: Activation functions are essential for transforming input activations into classifications, determining whether a neuron is activated and if a data point belongs to class 0 or class 1. The process involves input Z, which is used to make classification decisions. An example discussed is the binary classification using a step function, where the function outputs a decision based on whether Z is greater than or equal to zero or less than zero.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why we need to have an activation function or like one example of why we need to have an activation function because we want to use input activations to turn them into classifications, into decisions, whether the neuron is activated or not, or decisions into whether the data point belongs to class 0 or class 1. So this is kind of a simple way. Again, that's the kind of a way that the activation function is working. Input Z is going in. Then it decides to do the classification for us. children.title: Binary Classification and Step Function Explanation
children.summary: The classification process utilizes a step function, which creates a linear decision boundary, allowing for perfect classification of linearly separable data. The discussion highlights the similarity between the step function and logistic regression, emphasizing that both involve calculating a linear component (z) and applying an activation function to derive probabilities. The lecture also raises a question about the omission of the step function in earlier discussions on logistic regression.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then the way that it does the classification for us is that because the step function has a linear decision boundary, because now the characteristic for this z is that it's a linear model that I have right here. Then I have a line between the two classes. And because my data set was linearly separable, then I can actually directly cut through the two classes. Then I will have a perfect classification between the two classes. Very interesting stuff. So, this is step function is very similar to something that you've seen before. And actually, Joy and I were talking about when we were teaching you logistic regression, why did we not tell you about the step function? We could just simply say that when you're doing binary classification, you do activation, right? Because logistic regression also had the calculation of that linear part, because you had to actually calculate z and then pass z to the signal function to get the probabilities. leaf_chunk.audio: And then the way that it does the classification for us is that because the step function has a linear decision boundary, because now the characteristic for this z is that it's a linear model that I have right here. Then I have a line between the two classes. And because my data set was linearly separable, then I can actually directly cut through the two classes. Then I will have a perfect classification between the two classes. Very interesting stuff. So, this is step function is very similar to something that you've seen before. And actually, Joy and I were talking about when we were teaching you logistic regression, why did we not tell you about the step function? We could just simply say that when you're doing binary classification, you do activation, right? Because logistic regression also had the calculation of that linear part, because you had to actually calculate z and then pass z to the signal function to get the probabilities.

children.title: Sigmoid Function and Neural Network Activation
children.summary: The segment discusses the limitations of the step function in classification tasks due to its non-differentiability, which complicates gradient descent optimization. The sigmoid function is introduced as a differentiable alternative that maps inputs to a range between zero and one, allowing for probabilistic interpretations with a threshold of 0.5 for classification. The importance of activation functions, particularly the sigmoid, in neurons for binary classification is emphasized. Additionally, the limitations of individual neurons are addressed, leading to the necessity of neural networks. An analogy between neurons and logical gates is made, and different activation functions are explored, highlighting their roles in enhancing the expressiveness of artificial neurons and the significance of deep neural networks for multi-class classification and feature detection.

children.title: Sigmoid Function in Neural Classifiers
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the limitations of the step function in classification tasks, particularly its non-differentiability at certain points, which complicates gradient descent optimization. In contrast, the sigmoid function is introduced as a solution, providing a differentiable transition between classes zero and one. The sigmoid function maps inputs to a range between zero and one, allowing for probabilistic interpretations of outputs, where a threshold of 0.5 is used to classify inputs. The segment emphasizes the role of activation functions, such as the sigmoid, in neurons for binary classification and optimizing parameters to achieve reasonable probability outputs.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One way of simplifying that was that, what if we told you that, OK, go ahead and calculate z, pass it through the step function, get two classes, class zero and class one. What's the issue with the step function that we did not teach you that I'm we instead talk about the sigma function. The issue is that if I was using that classifier and I wanted to train a classifier in terms of figuring out whether the parameters theta that I have in order to calculate z equal to the dot product within the theta vector and the x vector. The issue when you're calculating the gradient descent function is that this function is not differentiable in all points. So the derivative at zero is not the fun. So that's part of the reason that we don't necessarily use this as the function in practice because we can actually optimize using that. The other situation is that if you relax this kind of transition between the class of zero and class of one, we could actually come up with the sigmoid function. So this is another way of us trying to make sure that at zero the function is differentiable, but it is still does the same thing that it maps the inputs into the range of zero and one. So it is between zero and one. It is a probability. if we use the threshold of 0.5, anything above it is going to be classified as class 1, anything below it is going to be classified as class 0. So again, a neuron had a summation unit. It has an activation function. We talked about the activation function. It can be just a simple linear function. It can also be a step function or it can be the sigmoid function. If we use sigmoid function as the activation function, the output of each neuron is going to be between 0 and 1. It's a probability. So we can actually try to optimize the tetavalu's in a neuron so that it outputs probabilities that are reasonable and can cut through between the two classes that we have and do the binary classification for a given neuron that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: One way of simplifying that was that, what if we told you that, OK, go ahead and calculate z, pass it through the step function, get two classes, class zero and class one. What's the issue with the step function that we did not teach you that I'm we instead talk about the sigma function. The issue is that if I was using that classifier and I wanted to train a classifier in terms of figuring out whether the parameters theta that I have in order to calculate z equal to the dot product within the theta vector and the x vector. The issue when you're calculating the gradient descent function is that this function is not differentiable in all points. So the derivative at zero is not the fun. So that's part of the reason that we don't necessarily use this as the function in practice because we can actually optimize using that. The other situation is that if you relax this kind of transition between the class of zero and class of one, we could actually come up with the sigmoid function. So this is another way of us trying to make sure that at zero the function is differentiable, but it is still does the same thing that it maps the inputs into the range of zero and one. So it is between zero and one. It is a probability. if we use the threshold of 0.5, anything above it is going to be classified as class 1, anything below it is going to be classified as class 0. So again, a neuron had a summation unit. It has an activation function. We talked about the activation function. It can be just a simple linear function. It can also be a step function or it can be the sigmoid function. If we use sigmoid function as the activation function, the output of each neuron is going to be between 0 and 1. It's a probability. So we can actually try to optimize the tetavalu's in a neuron so that it outputs probabilities that are reasonable and can cut through between the two classes that we have and do the binary classification for a given neuron that we have.

children.title: Neurons, Activation Functions, and Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the limitations of individual neurons and the rationale for advancing to neural networks. It introduces the analogy between neurons and logical gates, emphasizing the need for more complex structures to overcome these limitations. The segment also explores different activation functions, particularly the step function and sigmoid function, highlighting their roles in neural networks and the expressiveness of artificial neurons. The importance of deep neural networks is mentioned, setting the stage for further exploration of their capabilities in multi-class classification and feature detection.

children.title: Simulating AND Gate with Neurons and Functionality
children.summary: The segment discusses simulating an AND gate using a simple neuron model, where the output y equals 1 only when both inputs x1 and x2 are 1. It visualizes the feature space, indicating that y is 1 in a specific region and 0 elsewhere. The neuron construction involves a step function activation with a zero threshold, incorporating bias and inputs. The goal is to determine parameters (theta values) that define the decision boundary separating the two classes. Additionally, the discussion covers classifying inputs for the Boolean AND gate, with values above a threshold classified as class one and below as class zero. An example with parameters such as theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = -0.5, and theta_2 = -0.5 is provided, illustrating how these parameters influence output Y for different input values of X1 and X2.

children.title: Simulating an AND Gate with Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses simulating an AND gate using a simple neuron model in machine learning. It explains the function taking inputs x1 and x2, mapping them to output y, which equals 1 only when both inputs are 1. The feature space is visualized, showing that y is 1 in a specific region and 0 elsewhere. The construction of the neuron involves a step function activation with a threshold of zero, incorporating bias and the inputs. The goal is to determine the appropriate parameters (tetah values) that define the decision boundary separating the two classes.

Audio: And if that's a teaser into the next part of the five minutes of the lecture that we're going to spend on. So the first thing that we want to simulate is that what if I have that function? Again, anything in machine learning is a function. So let's say that I have a function that takes in the values x1 and x2, and then it tries to map it to the output y. And the output y happens to be corresponding to the AND gate that I have. Right? So notice that the y value is only equal to 1 when both x1 and x2 are equal to 1 and in other places it's equal to zero. If I draw what's happening in my feature space is that if I plot x1 versus x2 and you notice that only in this case, y is equal to one and in other cases, y is equal to zero that I showed with the red dots. What I want to actually do is to build a simple neuron with the step function activation that has a threshold of zero obviously with those parameters that I have, right? So we always have the bias term. We always have all of the inputs going in. So I have input x1, I have input x2, because these are the two inputs that I had into the function. leaf_chunk.audio: And if that's a teaser into the next part of the five minutes of the lecture that we're going to spend on. So the first thing that we want to simulate is that what if I have that function? Again, anything in machine learning is a function. So let's say that I have a function that takes in the values x1 and x2, and then it tries to map it to the output y. And the output y happens to be corresponding to the AND gate that I have. Right? So notice that the y value is only equal to 1 when both x1 and x2 are equal to 1 and in other places it's equal to zero. If I draw what's happening in my feature space is that if I plot x1 versus x2 and you notice that only in this case, y is equal to one and in other cases, y is equal to zero that I showed with the red dots. What I want to actually do is to build a simple neuron with the step function activation that has a threshold of zero obviously with those parameters that I have, right? So we always have the bias term. We always have all of the inputs going in. So I have input x1, I have input x2, because these are the two inputs that I had into the function. Then we have the corresponding tetah values, tetah1 and tetah2. What is the combination of tetah0, tetah1 and tetah2 values that is going to give me the exact. and function that we have here. So take a look at this that Kind of intuition behind it is that I'm trying to find the characteristics for the line that cuts between the two classes.

children.title: Functional Completeness of Boolean AND Gate
children.summary: The discussion focuses on classifying inputs for a Boolean AND gate, where values above a certain threshold are classified as class one and those below as class zero. An example is provided with parameters for the AND gate, including theta values such as theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = -0.5, and theta_2 = -0.5. The slide illustrates the function and output combinations for different input values of X1 and X2, emphasizing how these parameters affect the output Y.

children.title: AND Gate Parameters and Neuron Output Calculation
children.summary: Discussion on potential parameters for an AND gate highlights multiple correct solutions, including combinations like theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = -0.5, theta_2 = -0.5, and variations thereof. Four equations are derived to determine the values of theta for a neuron model, emphasizing that for inputs x1 = 1 and x2 = 0, the output must be less than zero to remain within the step function's correct side. Conditions for the output of a step function in neural networks are outlined, specifying that theta_0 + theta_1 * x1 must be less than zero for an output of zero, while the output is one when theta_0 + theta_1 + theta_2 * x2 is greater than or equal to zero. The derivation of equations concerning neural inputs is discussed, focusing on conditions where the sum of parameters multiplied by inputs results in values less than zero. The segment concludes with a probabilistic view on solutions, noting that the step function outputs one only when z is greater than or equal to zero, indicating multiple solutions exist for the equations with three unknowns.

children.title: Example Parameters for AND Gate
children.summary: Discussion on potential parameters for an AND gate, highlighting that multiple correct solutions exist. Suggested parameters include theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = -0.5, theta_2 = -0.5, and variations such as theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = 0.5, theta_2 = 0.5, and others. The importance of the bias term in determining these parameters is emphasized.

children.title: Equations for Neuron Output Calculation
children.summary: Four equations are derived to determine the values of Teta 0, Teta 1, and Teta 2 for a neuron model. By setting inputs x1 to 1 and x2 to 0, the output must equal 0, indicating that the weighted combination of inputs must be less than 0 to remain on the correct side of the step function. Example parameters for an AND gate are provided, including various combinations of theta values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right? So we have Teta 0, Teta 1 and Teta 2 that are unknowns. But I can actually write four equations in order to figure out, how can I actually come up with why values for all of these four rows that I have? Right? So I'm going to actually write down four equations there. So I'm assuming that, okay, if I go ahead and pass, say, x1 be equal to 1 and x2 equal to 0 into this neuron and then calculate the combination of those inputs that I pass, I need to make sure that the output is equal to 0. And I want to make sure that the output is equal to 0. This means that I need to be on this side of this step function. That weighted combination needs to be less than 0. So I will write this out. children.title: Activation Function Conditions in Neural Networks
children.summary: The conditions for the output of a step function in a neural network are discussed. Specifically, for the output to be zero, the equation theta_0 + theta_1 * x1 must be less than zero, while for the output to be one, the equation theta_0 + theta_1 + theta_2 * x2 should be greater than or equal to zero. This establishes the necessary activation thresholds for the neuron.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'll say that, OK, I have theta 0 plus theta 1 times x1 in this case is equal to 1 plus theta 2 times x2, which in this case is equal to 0. This all should be less than 0 for me to be able to output a 0 value. because that's what my step function is actually going to do. So I'm going to simplify this, theta zero plus theta one should be less than zero. Kind of similarly, I can write that as theta zero plus theta one plus theta two should be greater than or equal to zero. Right? Because this is the case that why should be equal to one? So I should have an activation that's greater than or equal to zero. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Equation Derivation for Neural Inputs
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of equations related to neural inputs, specifically focusing on the conditions where the sum of parameters (theta) multiplied by inputs results in values less than zero. The equations include theta 0 plus theta 1 times x plus theta 2 times another input being less than zero, and a condition where theta 0 plus theta 2 is also less than zero. The speaker questions how these conditions relate to achieving values greater than or equal to zero for another line.

children.title: Step Function and Probabilistic View on Solutions
children.summary: The output of the step function is one only when z is greater than or equal to zero; otherwise, it is zero. This leads to multiple solutions for the equations with three unknowns, indicating that various lines can separate two classes correctly. However, only one line will maximize the loss function. Additionally, parameters teta-0, teta-1, and teta-2 must be positive, but not excessively so, to ensure that their summation remains positive.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is because my output is equal to one. This is the only case that the output is equal to one and based on the step function, we said that if z is greater than or equal to zero, the output is equal to one and if z is less than zero, the output is equal to zero. So that's why for all of those other lines that I have zero, what I'm putting is, so these are all zeroes, so they all need to be less than zero, less than zero, less than zero except for the case that output is equal to one. Great question. Okay, so let me actually solve it to show you what's happening. So you can see from this line that your tet to zero could not have been equal to zero. So that's how I kind of I could tell quickly that the choice that most of the students picked was not correct. So hypothetically, let's say that I go ahead and set that to zero equal to negative one. Right? And again, notice here that I have four equations, three unknowns. So that's the degree, that's the next degree of freedom that I have. That's why I have more than one correct solution here. So I can put multiple different lines between all of these, between these two classes. And all of those lines would be correct. But we're going to take a look at the probabilistic view of this and we'll see that only one of those lines is going to maximize the loss function that we have. And that's, I think what Joey is going to go over and show you what's going to happen. So let's take a look at this case. So here in this case, I'm saying that, okay, the kind of another information that I'm getting here is that I need to have tet-0, tet-1 and tet-2 to be a positive value. So that's kind of a hint for me that tet-1 and tet-2 should be a positive value. But not so positive that only one of them by itself can push the summation of of teta 0 plus teta 2 to be a positive value. So I want something positive, but not so positive. So for example, here I would say that, OK, if teta 2 is equal to, for example, 0.5, and if also teta 1 is also, so I'm going to write it here, if teta 1 is also equal to 0.5, so I'm going to highlight all of these. for you, teta 1.5, teta 2.5, and then teta 1, negative 1. So you would be able to see that all of these equations are satisfied because then this big summation that was of concern here is going to be exactly equal to zero. leaf_chunk.audio: This is because my output is equal to one. This is the only case that the output is equal to one and based on the step function, we said that if z is greater than or equal to zero, the output is equal to one and if z is less than zero, the output is equal to zero. So that's why for all of those other lines that I have zero, what I'm putting is, so these are all zeroes, so they all need to be less than zero, less than zero, less than zero except for the case that output is equal to one. Great question. Okay, so let me actually solve it to show you what's happening. So you can see from this line that your tet to zero could not have been equal to zero. So that's how I kind of I could tell quickly that the choice that most of the students picked was not correct. So hypothetically, let's say that I go ahead and set that to zero equal to negative one. Right? And again, notice here that I have four equations, three unknowns. So that's the degree, that's the next degree of freedom that I have. That's why I have more than one correct solution here. So I can put multiple different lines between all of these, between these two classes. And all of those lines would be correct. But we're going to take a look at the probabilistic view of this and we'll see that only one of those lines is going to maximize the loss function that we have. And that's, I think what Joey is going to go over and show you what's going to happen. So let's take a look at this case. So here in this case, I'm saying that, okay, the kind of another information that I'm getting here is that I need to have tet-0, tet-1 and tet-2 to be a positive value. So that's kind of a hint for me that tet-1 and tet-2 should be a positive value. But not so positive that only one of them by itself can push the summation of of teta 0 plus teta 2 to be a positive value. So I want something positive, but not so positive. So for example, here I would say that, OK, if teta 2 is equal to, for example, 0.5, and if also teta 1 is also, so I'm going to write it here, if teta 1 is also equal to 0.5, so I'm going to highlight all of these. for you, teta 1.5, teta 2.5, and then teta 1, negative 1. So you would be able to see that all of these equations are satisfied because then this big summation that was of concern here is going to be exactly equal to zero.

children.title: Symmetry in Equations and Functional Completeness
children.summary: The discussion highlights the concept of symmetry in equations, specifically referencing tetah0 as a constraining equation and noting that tetah1 and tetat2 act as symmetric variables. It emphasizes that the equations mentioned are not separate due to their symmetric nature. Additionally, the segment introduces the concept of functional completeness in the context of Boolean AND operations, illustrating how different variable combinations can affect the output.

Audio: Right? So for example, tetah0 being a negative value is a constraining kind of equation, right? But then what you would see is that then this is going to be a limiting equation right here, but then tetah1 and tetat2 are actually also acting as symmetric kind of variables. So in that sense, this equation right here, and this equation right here, are not two separate equations. because there's symmetric. Great. Good question. Alright, so that was kind of a warm-up. children.title: Building an OR Gate with Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses constructing an OR gate using a neuron model, encouraging experimentation with optimal parameters for theta values (theta_0, theta_1, theta_2). It suggests using symmetry and guessing techniques similar to those used for the AND gate to determine the necessary parameters for the OR gate.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we were able to build a neuron that actually does the AND gate for us. Now let's take a look at this other one. So what if I want to build an OR gate? So this is all on you to actually experiment and then figure out what are the optimum parameters for tetas 0, teta 1 and teta 2 so that I can build and or get. So this is another slide of question. Go ahead, spend few minutes on it. This is going to be a nice practice. You can use the same technique that I had. You can guess that is zero and then you can use kind of the same symmetric property between I'm going to touch the one and touch the two and then be able to find out what. leaf_chunk.audio: So we were able to build a neuron that actually does the AND gate for us. Now let's take a look at this other one. So what if I want to build an OR gate? So this is all on you to actually experiment and then figure out what are the optimum parameters for tetas 0, teta 1 and teta 2 so that I can build and or get. So this is another slide of question. Go ahead, spend few minutes on it. This is going to be a nice practice. You can use the same technique that I had. You can guess that is zero and then you can use kind of the same symmetric property between I'm going to touch the one and touch the two and then be able to find out what.

Participants provided various values, including theta0 = 0, theta0 = -0.5, and theta1 = 0.5, among others, to illustrate how these parameters can be configured.

children.title: Neural Network Activation and OR Gate Parameters
children.summary: Discussion on the importance of negative weights for neuron activation, specifically in the context of ensuring that a neuron does not activate when both inputs x1 and x2 are zero. Examples of parameters for an OR gate are provided, including theta values: theta0 = -1, theta1 = 2, and theta2 = 1, illustrating how these parameters contribute to the functional completeness of the OR operation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Oh! Values for PET-1 and PET-2. Let's go ahead and give this a try. So if I go ahead and say I think one suggestion was that Uh, that... One thing that I saw was that someone was suggesting that it was zero to be negative point four. This is because we just want to make sure that If I don't have activation on x1 and x2, if x1 and x2 are both equal to zero and no activation or no input, is coming through the input channels right there, I don't want the neuron to be activated. So that's why a negative weight for the tetra zero by itself is going to be very important. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Determining Weights for OR Gate Neuron
children.summary: To create a neuron that functions as an OR gate, it is essential to choose appropriate weights for inputs x1 and x2. The goal is to ensure that if at least one input is activated, the summation is positive. For instance, if x1 is activated and has a weight of 0.5, along with a negative weight of -0.4, the total must be greater than or equal to zero, which is satisfied with this choice. By determining optimal parameters, the neuron can accurately classify inputs into binary classes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But at the same time, we want to make sure that we pick the weights for x1 and x2 So that if at least one of them is activated, this summation is actually positive, right? So what's the value that I can pick for tet-1? So that when tet-1 is only, or the x-1 is only activated, and I sum it up with negative point four, it is greater than or equal to zero. So it can be 0.4 or above. So for example here if I pick 0.5 and here I pick 0.5 then I have all of the optimum values that I want in order to actually realize the or gate. So notice that again the task of creating a neuron is again for us to come up with the optimum parameters. And once we find the optimum parameters, then we have a function. And that function takes inputs and then generates the output perfectly and then it classifies points into classes of zeroes and months. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: But at the same time, we want to make sure that we pick the weights for x1 and x2 So that if at least one of them is activated, this summation is actually positive, right? So what's the value that I can pick for tet-1? So that when tet-1 is only, or the x-1 is only activated, and I sum it up with negative point four, it is greater than or equal to zero. So it can be 0.4 or above. So for example here if I pick 0.5 and here I pick 0.5 then I have all of the optimum values that I want in order to actually realize the or gate. So notice that again the task of creating a neuron is again for us to come up with the optimum parameters. And once we find the optimum parameters, then we have a function. And that function takes inputs and then generates the output perfectly and then it classifies points into classes of zeroes and months.

children.title: Constructing XOR Gate for Binary Classification
children.summary: The XOR gate outputs 1 when one input is true and the other is false, with 0 in all other cases. To create a neuron representing the XOR function for binary classification, separate classifiers for different classes are required, utilizing multiple neurons and possibly different activation functions. A minimum of three neurons is needed to construct an XOR gate, where each neuron classifies different linear boundaries, allowing effective separation of values in the truth table. The discussion also highlights the implementation of neural networks to achieve non-linear decision boundaries by combining multiple linear classifiers, demonstrating how to build classifiers for various logic gates like AND, OR, and XOR.

children.title: Building an XOR Gate for Binary Classification
children.summary: The XOR gate outputs a value of 1 when only one of its two inputs is active, specifically in the cases where one input is true and the other is false. In all other scenarios, the output is 0. A discussion is initiated on how to construct a neuron that can represent the XOR function, particularly in the context of binary classification between class zero and class one, noting that these classes have different structures compared to AND and OR gates.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But now let's take a look at x or gate. So for the x or gate, what's happening is that the x or value is going output is going to be equal to 1 in the cases that only one of the two is active. So again, if you're looking at the output is that only for this case and for this case, the output is equal to 1 and in other in all other cases, the output is equal to 0. So a question for you is that How can we build the X or Gate? Just brainstorm ideas. So I have an x or gate. I want to do a binary classification between class zero and class one. But now there is an issue that class zero and class one are kind of differently structured than what I saw in the AND gate and OR gate. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: But now let's take a look at x or gate. So for the x or gate, what's happening is that the x or value is going output is going to be equal to 1 in the cases that only one of the two is active. So again, if you're looking at the output is that only for this case and for this case, the output is equal to 1 and in other in all other cases, the output is equal to 0. So a question for you is that How can we build the X or Gate? Just brainstorm ideas. So I have an x or gate. I want to do a binary classification between class zero and class one. But now there is an issue that class zero and class one are kind of differently structured than what I saw in the AND gate and OR gate.

children.title: Building Neurons for XOR Classification
children.summary: To create a classifier for the XOR problem, it is necessary to develop separate classifiers for different classes represented by the data points. This involves using multiple neurons and possibly changing activation functions. The discussion emphasizes the need to effectively separate the classes by utilizing combinations of logical gates (AND, OR) and understanding the role of parameters like negative theta zero in neuron activation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Negative theta zero, changing activation function, combination of n and or gate. neurons, two functions. So that's kind of the idea. Again, I give you the hint on the plot that if I want to actually be able to build a classifier, I need to have a classifier for one of these green kind of notes separately and I need to have another classifier for the top green note separately in order to be able to separate out my classes, my data points to be be like the class one. children.title: Minimum Neurons Required for XOR Gate
children.summary: To construct an XOR gate, a minimum of three neurons is necessary. Each neuron acts as a classifier for different linear boundaries. Specifically, one neuron classifies one input line, another neuron classifies the second input line, and a third neuron combines the outputs of the first two. This setup is essential to effectively separate the values in the truth table for the XOR function, utilizing linear classifiers to achieve the desired output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So then the other question is that how many neurons do I need a minimum in order to find out, in order to build this x-ore gate? I'm definitely needing more than one. Who is closed, but not closed enough? or but there is a minimum. I need to have the minimum of three nodes in order or three neurons in order to build this function. I need to have one classifier for one line. I need to have another classifier for the other line. And then I need to have a way of combining the two. So combining the two is this turd line right here that is actually going to actually need to be a classifier or another neuron that I need to build. So because I have tree linear kind of lines or linear classifiers here, I will need at least minimum tree neurons. So the way that it works is that, and the way, and why we need to actually have tree neurons is that if you actually try to separate out this truce table function using this chunk of normal form, so what's happening is that I can actually say that I'm separating this value of 1, which corresponds to this node right here in one truce table. So I'm going to build actually the classifier right here for this is specific truce table. And then I'm going to build another classifier that's going to actually give me that... Classifier for this other node, right? So this is another true staple and then what I need is actually taking the or between the two. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

Who is closed, but not closed enough? or but there is a minimum. I need to have the minimum of three nodes in order or three neurons in order to build this function. I need to have one classifier for one line. I need to have another classifier for the other line. And then I need to have a way of combining the two. So combining the two is this turd line right here that is actually going to actually need to be a classifier or another neuron that I need to build. So because I have tree linear kind of lines or linear classifiers here, I will need at least minimum tree neurons. So the way that it works is that, and the way, and why we need to actually have tree neurons is that if you actually try to separate out this truce table function using this chunk of normal form, so what's happening is that I can actually say that I'm separating this value of 1, which corresponds to this node right here in one truce table. So I'm going to build actually the classifier right here for this is specific truce table. And then I'm going to build another classifier that's going to actually give me that... children.title: Neural Networks and Logic Gates Implementation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on classifying inputs using two classifiers to determine the output of a neuron, emphasizing that each neuron has a linear decision boundary. To achieve a non-linear decision boundary, multiple linear boundaries must be combined, which is the foundation of neural networks. A demonstration is introduced where various logic gates (AND, OR, XOR) are created to illustrate how classifiers for these gates can be built, along with the ability to adjust noise levels in the samples.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I would say that either you have to be classified into class one using the bottom classifier or you need to be classified into class one using the top classifier and then I'm going to take the or between the two and if you meet at least one of them then the output is going to be equal to one. So that's why you would see that this is one neuron. This right here is one neuron and then I would need to have another neuron in order to combine them and that's the minimum. And that's where the idea of neural network comes into play that there is the limitation that each neuron has because each neuron has a linear decision boundary. And what I need to do is if my decision boundary becomes non-linear, I need to combine multiple different linear decision boundaries together in order to build a non-linear decision boundary. So that's exactly what's happening. So let me quickly go through a demo and show you how this is actually going to happen. Let me restart this quickly. Okay, so let's take a look. So I'm here actually going to create a bunch of different gates. So I'm creating and gate or gate and x or gate to show you how it actually going to build classifiers for each one of these different gates that we have. The other thing that we do is actually showing you that we can come up with number of samples, we can pick the gate and then we can change the noise level. So for example, here if I go ahead and say that, I want AND gate with this specific noise level. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X
