leaf_chunk.audio: I have a bunch of strong connections and a bunch of weak connections and the idea of weak connection is that those neurons or those inputs, whatever they say, we ignore. We don't listen to them. We don't look at them and then we pay attention to the strong connections. Right, so the term attention that you'll hear probably in deep learning is kind of coming from the same idea that like which area or what feature we're paying attention to So here we're picking dark area in the specific region, so that's kind of generally one feature detector. So you can think about each one of those neurons as actually small feature detectors that we have. But each one of these features are actually very simple. What we need is a very complicated set of features, or a complicated set of feature detectors in order to complete the job. So for example, if you're looking at all of the different variety of handwritten digits that we have. In order to be able to do this 9-way classification or 10-way classification that we have, we need to have, for example, detect vertical lines. If there is a vertical line, there is more probability that, for example, a digit is 1 compared to a digit being equal to 0. That's how you can think about why those features are important. For example, we might need to detect horizontal lines. If you have a horizontal line, it's more likely for your digit to be, for example, a digit of two compared to a digit of one. So you can think about those features being very important in aggregate when you're trying to find patterns to distinguish between the classes.

children.title: Position Invariance in Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the ability of neural networks to detect features such as horizontal lines, vertical lines, and circles in images. It highlights the challenge of position dependency in feature detection, where specific detectors are tied to certain parts of the image. The question raised is how to achieve position invariance, allowing the network to recognize features regardless of their location within the image.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then obviously, we can also find circles. Like one doesn't have circle, but two or nine or six, they can have circles in those images. But the issue is that I have a small feature detectors. I showed you that, OK, I can detect a horizontal line. I can detect a vertical line, I can detect kind of a circle or black area in the rich region. But they are always positioned specific, because I was connecting to the first seven pixels. I was connecting to the second seven pixels, for example. So it was always position dependent. How can I actually make it position invariant? Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Position Invariance and Layer Complexity in Neural Networks
children.summary: Position invariance in neural networks is achieved through multiple layers of neurons, beginning with weak learners that identify basic features such as horizontal lines. Each subsequent layer builds upon these features, allowing for the detection of more complex patterns regardless of their position in the input. For example, a neuron in the second layer can recognize horizontal lines, vertical lines, and circles, demonstrating a hierarchical approach that enables the network to learn increasingly abstract representations.

children.title: Position Invariance and Layer Complexity in Neural Networks
children.summary: Position invariance in neural networks is achieved through multiple layers of neurons, starting with weak learners that detect basic features like horizontal lines. Each subsequent layer builds on these features, enabling the detection of more complex patterns independent of their positions in the input. For instance, a neuron in the second layer can recognize horizontal lines, vertical lines, and circles, illustrating a hierarchical approach that allows the network to learn increasingly abstract representations.

children.title: Position Invariance and Layer Complexity in Neural Networks
children.summary: Position invariance in neural networks is achieved by using multiple layers of neurons, where the first layer consists of weak learners that detect basic features like horizontal lines. Each subsequent layer builds on these features, allowing for the detection of more complex patterns without being tied to specific positions in the input. For example, a neuron in the second layer can recognize horizontal lines regardless of their location, as well as other features like vertical lines and circles. This hierarchical approach enables the network to learn increasingly abstract representations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In order to make a position in variance, so you can think about having the input layer going into the first layer, the first layer being a bunch of different weak learners. Or they are detecting lower level features for us. So I can build for example one neuron that detects the first line or first horizontal line second horizontal line third horizontal line and so on and so forth. and then I can add position invariance by taking or between them. I can say that do you have a horizontal line at all or not and we know how we can build or gate. So that's why we need to add layers. You can see that the neuron that I have in the second layer is more complex than the neuron in the first layer. Because the second one, not only that it detects the horizontal line but is also not positioned as specific. I can do exactly the same thing with other features. I can detect vertical lines anywhere, I can detect circles, any verse on and so forth. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: In order to make a position in variance, so you can think about having the input layer going into the first layer, the first layer being a bunch of different weak learners. Or they are detecting lower level features for us. So I can build for example one neuron that detects the first line or first horizontal line second horizontal line third horizontal line and so on and so forth. and then I can add position invariance by taking or between them. I can say that do you have a horizontal line at all or not and we know how we can build or gate. So that's why we need to add layers. You can see that the neuron that I have in the second layer is more complex than the neuron in the first layer. Because the second one, not only that it detects the horizontal line but is also not positioned as specific. I can do exactly the same thing with other features. I can detect vertical lines anywhere, I can detect circles, any verse on and so forth.

children.title: Feature Detection and Multi-Class Classification in Neural Networks
children.summary: Successive layers in neural networks learn to detect higher-level features from input data, enhancing classification accuracy by focusing on distinct features like shapes. Multi-class classification utilizes a softmax function to normalize activations into probabilities for effective class comparison. Deep neural networks offer advantages such as increased complexity of learnable functions through multiple hidden layers, allowing for the capture of non-linear patterns. The universality theorem indicates that a sufficiently large single hidden layer can approximate any function, while stacking layers can reduce the size of each layer required for efficient function realization, simplifying training and relating to logic circuits.

children.title: Feature Detection and Multi-Class Classification in Neural Networks
children.summary: Successive layers in neural networks learn to detect higher-level features from input data, which enhances classification accuracy by focusing on distinct features like shapes. Multi-class classification employs a softmax function to normalize activations into probabilities for effective class comparison. The advantages of deep neural networks include increased complexity of learnable functions through multiple hidden layers, enabling the capture of non-linear patterns. The universality theorem suggests that a sufficiently large single hidden layer can approximate any function, while stacking layers can reduce the size of each layer needed for efficient function realization, simplifying training and relating to logic circuits.

children.title: Feature Detection, Multi-Class Classification, and Deep Neural Networks
children.summary: Successive layers in neural networks learn to detect higher-level features from input data, such as shapes like vertical lines or loops, enhancing classification accuracy by focusing on distinct features. Multi-class classification utilizes a softmax function to normalize activations into probabilities, allowing for effective comparison between classes. The advantages of deep neural networks are highlighted, noting that multiple hidden layers increase the complexity of learnable functions and can capture non-linear patterns. The universality theorem is referenced, indicating that a sufficiently large single hidden layer can approximate any function, while stacking layers can reduce the size of each layer needed for efficient function realization, simplifying training and relating to logic circuits.

children.title: Feature Detection in Neural Networks
children.summary: Successive layers in neural networks can learn to detect higher-level features by extracting relevant information from input data, such as identifying specific shapes like vertical lines or loops. This process involves combining various weights to improve classification accuracy, allowing the network to differentiate between similar digits, such as six and nine, by focusing on distinct features while ignoring others.

children.title: Multi-Class Classification and Softmax Function
children.summary: Multi-class classification involves normalizing activations to form a probability function, allowing for comparison between classes. Instead of using independent probabilities from a sigmoid function, a softmax layer is employed to transform the output into a set of probabilities. This process facilitates the evaluation of the likelihood of each class based on the activations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the next part is actually going to talk about why, how we can actually do multi-class classification. The idea is that we have multi-different classes and what we need to do is we take all of these activations. And instead of using those activations directly, we will normalize those activations so that those activations form a probability function. And then you can actually compare the probability of class 0 versus probability of class 1 and so on and so forth. So what's happening is that instead of me passing each one of those activations in the output layer to a sigmoid function and get independent probabilities, then what I'll have is I will actually have something that's called softmax layer. That is actually going to take these activations in the input. It will apply a certain function called softmax function. Again, I'm not going to go over that. That will turn the output layer into a set of probabilities as opposed to individual probabilities. Video: 1: V

leaf_chunk.audio: So the next part is actually going to talk about why, how we can actually do multi-class classification. The idea is that we have multi-different classes and what we need to do is we take all of these activations. And instead of using those activations directly, we will normalize those activations so that those activations form a probability function. And then you can actually compare the probability of class 0 versus probability of class 1 and so on and so forth. So what's happening is that instead of me passing each one of those activations in the output layer to a sigmoid function and get independent probabilities, then what I'll have is I will actually have something that's called softmax layer. That is actually going to take these activations in the input. It will apply a certain function called softmax function. Again, I'm not going to go over that. That will turn the output layer into a set of probabilities as opposed to individual probabilities.

children.title: Deep Neural Networks and Function Complexity
children.summary: The segment discusses the advantages of deep neural networks, emphasizing that adding multiple hidden layers increases the complexity of the functions they can learn, allowing for the capture of more non-linear patterns. It references the universality theorem, which states that a sufficiently large single hidden layer can approximate any function mapping an n-dimensional input to an m-dimensional output. However, to realize complex functions efficiently, stacking multiple hidden layers reduces the required size of each layer, leading to fewer neurons and parameters, which simplifies training. The connection to logic circuits is also mentioned, where using multiple layers can decrease the number of gates needed to build functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now we can compare them a little bit more. Concretely, that is more likely for the data point that we have to belong to class 1 as opposed to class 2 because the probability is 88% as opposed to 12%. So. The next piece is kind of simple. The idea of, now you know that the idea of deep neural network is that we add multiple different hidden layers. If we add multiple different hidden layers, the complexity of the function that we're going to train is going to increase. And then we are going to be able to learn more non-linear patterns. The general idea comes from universality term in universality term. They're saying that we can learn any function that maps an end-dimensional input to an M-dimensional output. Using one hidden one neural network with one hidden layer that is large enough so the look the neural that that hidden layer should be large enough for us to be able to realize that function. But the idea is that if we go ahead and want to realize any complex function, the size of that layer is going to grow very big and it's going to be a very exponential. So the idea that we'll use is we'll actually try to break down this entire fact, hidden layer, and stack them together and make it a deep neural network. And if you actually go ahead and take a look at the visualization that I link here, it's very useful for you to get some ideas. So we use ideas from logic circuit. Then in logic circuit, we can actually build any function with two layers. But if we actually stack them into multiple different layers, the number of gates that we're actually going to use is going to be reduced. So that's also the same thing that we know we can realize any function with one hidden layer of large size or large number of neurons. But if we actually stack them together, we're going to actually require less number of neurons and less number of neurons means less number of parameters that we have. If we have less number of parameters, their training is going to be simpler, and then we're going to require less data to do the training for us. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So now we can compare them a little bit more. Concretely, that is more likely for the data point that we have to belong to class 1 as opposed to class 2 because the probability is 88% as opposed to 12%. So. The next piece is kind of simple. The idea of, now you know that the idea of deep neural network is that we add multiple different hidden layers. If we add multiple different hidden layers, the complexity of the function that we're going to train is going to increase. And then we are going to be able to learn more non-linear patterns. The general idea comes from universality term in universality term. They're saying that we can learn any function that maps an end-dimensional input to an M-dimensional output. Using one hidden one neural network with one hidden layer that is large enough so the look the neural that that hidden layer should be large enough for us to be able to realize that function. But the idea is that if we go ahead and want to realize any complex function, the size of that layer is going to grow very big and it's going to be a very exponential. So the idea that we'll use is we'll actually try to break down this entire fact, hidden layer, and stack them together and make it a deep neural network. And if you actually go ahead and take a look at the visualization that I link here, it's very useful for you to get some ideas. So we use ideas from logic circuit. Then in logic circuit, we can actually build any function with two layers. But if we actually stack them into multiple different layers, the number of gates that we're actually going to use is going to be reduced. So that's also the same thing that we know we can realize any function with one hidden layer of large size or large number of neurons. But if we actually stack them together, we're going to actually require less number of neurons and less number of neurons means less number of parameters that we have. If we have less number of parameters, their training is going to be simpler, and then we're going to require less data to do the training for us.

children.title: Neural Networks History and PyTorch Implementation
children.summary: The historical development of neural networks is outlined, noting early applications in the 1990s, a decline in interest in the early 2000s, and a resurgence in the 2010s, with current widespread use. Scikit-learn's lack of support for neural networks is mentioned. A toy dataset of concentric circles is introduced for a binary classification task, demonstrating non-linear separability. The creation of 32-bit precision tensors in PyTorch for GPU processing is explained, including moving tensors to a GPU. The segment covers logistic regression models with defined layers, the 'init' function for component definition, and the 'forward' function for output computation. Transitioning from binary to multi-class classification using cross-entropy loss is discussed. The limitations of logistic regression lead to constructing a neural network with two hidden layers (2x8 and 8x8) culminating in a single output using a linear layer and tanh non-linearity, followed by a sigmoid function for probability output. Weight initialization, particularly the importance of non-zero initial weights and Xavier initialization, is emphasized. The optimization process is similar to logistic regression but requires careful weight setting. A practical demonstration shows a three hidden layer neural network learning decision boundaries and minimizing loss, with cross-entropy loss decreasing to 0.45. An interactive module allows experimentation with neural network architectures, activation functions, learning rates, and batch sizes, highlighting their significance in optimizing performance.

children.title: Neural Networks History and PyTorch Implementation
children.summary: The historical development of neural networks is outlined, noting early applications in the 1990s for tasks like reading checks and ATM transactions, followed by a decline in interest in the early 2000s due to scaling issues. The resurgence in the 2010s is highlighted, along with the current widespread use of neural networks, while mentioning that Scikit-learn does not support them. A toy dataset of two concentric circles is introduced to demonstrate a binary classification task that is not linearly separable. The creation of 32-bit precision tensors in PyTorch for GPU processing is covered, including the simplicity of moving tensors to a GPU. The segment explains the use of logistic regression models with defined layers, the 'init' function for model component definition, and the 'forward' function for output computation. Additionally, the transition from binary to multi-class classification using cross-entropy loss is discussed.

children.title: Historical Context and Neural Network Implementation
children.summary: The historical development of neural networks is discussed, highlighting their early 1990s applications in tasks like reading checks and ATM transactions, followed by a decline in research interest in the early 2000s due to scaling challenges. The resurgence of neural networks in the 2010s is noted, with their current prevalence in various applications, while mentioning that Scikit-learn does not support neural networks. A toy dataset of two concentric circles is introduced to illustrate a binary classification task that is not linearly separable. The segment covers the creation of 32-bit precision tensors in PyTorch for GPU processing, the ease of moving tensors to a GPU, and the use of logistic regression models with defined layers. The 'init' function is explained for model component definition, while the 'forward' function computes outputs. The transition from binary to multi-class classification using cross-entropy loss is also discussed.

children.title: Historical Context of Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion highlights the historical development of neural networks, noting their application in the early 1990s for tasks such as reading checks and ATM transactions. It mentions a decline in research interest in the early 2000s due to challenges in scaling models for complex functions. The resurgence of neural networks in the 2010s is emphasized, indicating their current prevalence in various applications, while also noting that Scikit-learn does not support neural networks and is therefore not used in this part of the class.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I want to quickly move us to the demo. We have 10-12 minutes to take a look at a very fun... Demel for us on Neural Network and PyTorch. Thank you very much. I love this stuff. I should get some background stories. It's kind of fun. So we're playing digits. Did you know we were using this stuff in like the early 1990s? In fact, when you're shipping, you know, sending a message in mail, or if you went to an ATM, that's an automated teller machine. It's a place where you stick your card and get cash out of. If you went to one of those, if you went to a positive check, which is something people used to do, you would read the check using this technology in the 90s. Early 90s is crazy. It's this old stuff. There was a period in time when it was terror like... That was a grad student. Early 2000s, when we didn't research this stuff, it wasn't trendy because the models didn't work. There's a classic result that said we couldn't scale these things to really, really complex function out making these things exponentially big. And then I guess in the 2010s, 2015 era, we switched back to everything as a neural net. And so now we do almost all the things we do in neural nets today. Scikit-learn is a cool package. It doesn't do neural nets, so. We don't use that for this part of the class. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So I want to quickly move us to the demo. We have 10-12 minutes to take a look at a very fun... Demel for us on Neural Network and PyTorch. Thank you very much. I love this stuff. I should get some background stories. It's kind of fun. So we're playing digits. Did you know we were using this stuff in like the early 1990s? In fact, when you're shipping, you know, sending a message in mail, or if you went to an ATM, that's an automated teller machine. It's a place where you stick your card and get cash out of. If you went to one of those, if you went to a positive check, which is something people used to do, you would read the check using this technology in the 90s. Early 90s is crazy. It's this old stuff. There was a period in time when it was terror like... That was a grad student. Early 2000s, when we didn't research this stuff, it wasn't trendy because the models didn't work. There's a classic result that said we couldn't scale these things to really, really complex function out making these things exponentially big. And then I guess in the 2010s, 2015 era, we switched back to everything as a neural net. And so now we do almost all the things we do in neural nets today. Scikit-learn is a cool package. It doesn't do neural nets, so. We don't use that for this part of the class.

children.title: Building a Neural Network for Non-Linearly Separable Data
children.summary: A toy dataset consisting of two concentric circles is introduced to illustrate a binary classification task that is not linearly separable. The segment discusses the creation of 32-bit precision tensors in PyTorch, emphasizing their use for GPU processing. The lecture also mentions the visualization code, although it is noted that this topic is outside the course scope.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm going to show you a notebook that I spent too much time tweaking. But it's fun notebook. So we're going to look at an interesting task. So a lot of this visualization code is way out of scope for this class. But if you want to learn about how to do fun visualizations, check it out. Let's just go ahead and run through this and set up our toy data set. So here's our new toy data set. Is this linearly separable? No. So let's go ahead and build a neural network to process this data. Perdelo, sir. I was performing a create tensors. One thing I was kind of neat here, I'm using these 32-bit tensors, these 32-bit precision tensors. The reason we work with tensors in PyTorch is often actually also been working on a GPU. def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

children.title: Using Tensors and Logistic Regression in PyTorch
children.summary: Tensors can be easily moved to a GPU in PyTorch, significantly speeding up operations like matrix multiplication. The logistic regression model utilizes a linear layer, which consists of a bias and weights. Instead of manually defining parameters, users can specify layers such as linear, convolutional, or transformer blocks in a single function, allowing for the construction of complex architectures with minimal code. The 'init' function defines the model components, while the 'forward' function computes the model's output from inputs, effectively utilizing the defined architecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm not showing this today, but it's very easy to tell PyTorch to move this tensor to a GPU, and then when I do this matrix multiply all this math, it will all run on my GPU many, many times faster. There's another reason we use tensors. Alright, time for the set. Choop. All right, so logistic regression model. Here I'm going to use the linear layer. So linear layer is a bias plus these weights. So it's a standard layer we've been talking about the whole way through. When we build networks, we don't typically write individual parameters today. We say, I want a linear layer, I want a con layer, I want these transformer blocks. So it's very easy to write one function. Like that to express really interesting architectures. And so there's architecture representing the fact that full collections of networks I can put as just one layer inside of my model. And so when you look at PyTorch code for, you know, state-the-art models, they might have like five or six lines where it says like this giant architecture connected to this other giant architecture. That's my model. So in these init functions, I'm defining the thing that my models have a linear layer, that's it, this is a registered regression model starting small. The forward function is where all the magic happens. This is the function that would take an input. And when I write this word function I'm telling the model I'm telling pie torch how to compute the output of this model it's building up the architecture not just you know defining the parameters but actually using them to make a producer result from some input. def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

[ ]:
# Logistic Regression Model
class LogisticRegressionModel(nn.Module) :
def __ init __ (self):
super(LogisticRegressionModel, self) ._ init __ ()
self. linear = nn.Linear(2, 1)
I
def forward(self, x):
p = torch.sigmoid(self.linear(x))
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
Step 2: Define the Loss
Here we are using cross entropy loss since we made the model return a probability for each class.

leaf_chunk.audio: I'm not showing this today, but it's very easy to tell PyTorch to move this tensor to a GPU, and then when I do this matrix multiply all this math, it will all run on my GPU many, many times faster. There's another reason we use tensors. Alright, time for the set. Choop. All right, so logistic regression model. Here I'm going to use the linear layer. So linear layer is a bias plus these weights. So it's a standard layer we've been talking about the whole way through. When we build networks, we don't typically write individual parameters today. We say, I want a linear layer, I want a con layer, I want these transformer blocks. So it's very easy to write one function. Like that to express really interesting architectures. And so there's architecture representing the fact that full collections of networks I can put as just one layer inside of my model. And so when you look at PyTorch code for, you know, state-the-art models, they might have like five or six lines where it says like this giant architecture connected to this other giant architecture. That's my model. So in these init functions, I'm defining the thing that my models have a linear layer, that's it, this is a registered regression model starting small. The forward function is where all the magic happens. This is the function that would take an input. And when I write this word function I'm telling the model I'm telling pie torch how to compute the output of this model it's building up the architecture not just you know defining the parameters but actually using them to make a producer result from some input.

children.title: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss and Multi-Class Predictions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the transition from binary to multi-class classification using cross-entropy loss. The speaker explains predicting two probabilities for binary classification: the probability of class zero (1 - p) and class one (p). The forward function is adjusted to output these two probabilities. The use of cross-entropy loss is emphasized as suitable for any number of classes. Additionally, there is a brief mention of visualization code related to the model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're using cross-entry P loss. We use the binary cross-entry loss before. Little subtleties. So before I was predicting one number, the probability of class one, now I'm going to start to predict the probability of class zero if you end, because I want to do multi-class. In binary, I have two classes. So I'm going to predict two probabilities. So you go back to my forward function, and compute p, but it would turn two numbers, one minus p and p. Right? Probably class zero, one minus P, probably class one, P. Now I can use a cross-entity loss, which will work for any number of dimensions. Sure any number of classes. Some more visualization code that I spent too much time on, so just it's pretty cool. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],

Probably class zero, one minus P, probably class one, P. Now I can use a cross-entity loss, which will work for any number of dimensions. Sure any number of classes. children.title: Gradient Descent and Optimizer in SGD
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in state-yard systems, utilizing data loaders to pull batches of data for computation. It reviews the process of initializing the optimizer, computing predictions using a model, and performing gradient descent during the backward step. The code illustrates how to handle data batches, shuffle them, and compute loss using cross-entropy loss, as well as defining a function to plot decision boundaries in PyTorch.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I won't walk through it. I do want to walk to the optimizer. So I taught SGD in this class. I promised you this was what we use in state-yard systems. So here is state-yard code doing this. As NARG has said before, we're using these data loaders. They're going to allow us to pull a batch of data into the computation. The loss of help is move stuff to GPU. So if we were computer GPU this would take care of that for us. I just want to walk to this code because this is sshd. It's review for for the exam. I loop over E-Pox, but it wasn't E-Pox. One pass to the data. Within each pot, it was in each epoch. I'm going to pull a batch of data out. So batch number, it's just an ID. And that's going to be a subset of my data, a little block of X and Ys. I'm shuffling it, doing SGD. I set my optimizer to zero so I'm initializing optimization step. I compute a prediction by just running my Model on X. and I can beat the loss. Where is gradient descent happening? This is gradient descent. in the backwards step. the lost. [ ]: def optimize_model(train_dataset,
test_dataset,
model, loss_fn,
pred_fig, loss_fig,
batch_size=64,
learning_rate = 0.01,
nepochs=50,
sleep_time=0.2):
import time
from torch. utils. data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), learning_rate)
test_loss_curve = []
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],

test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)

test_loss_curve = []
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches

# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

children.title: Gradient Computation and Adam Optimizer Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the computation of the gradient in a logistic regression model using the Adam Optimizer. It explains that the optimizer automatically calculates the gradient and updates the model parameters to ensure stability through additional parameters. The speaker demonstrates code for training a model with specified learning rates and epochs, utilizing a DataLoader for batching the training data. The process includes zeroing gradients, computing predictions and loss, performing backpropagation, and updating parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm computing the gradient. This is Calculus in 2025, right? We just say backward. This system automatically does all the math to compute the gradient. The optimizer. Is going to then take that gradient and do the weight update to my model parameters. Here I'm using the Adam Optimizer, which is Stady Optimizer, so it's Stady Art Code. This optimizer is to cast a gradient, but it's setting a bunch of extra parameters to make sure this thing is stable. Now we can try running this. That's my code. Moving through it now you can see... Oops, I... You can see... trying to solve a logistic regression model for this data set. The loss is going down. Why is it going down? I can't fit this state of logistic regression. children.title: Neural Network Structure, Initialization, and Visualization
children.summary: The discussion covers the limitations of logistic regression and the construction of a neural network with two hidden layers configured as 2x8 and 8x8, culminating in a single output value using a linear layer and tanh non-linearity, followed by a sigmoid function for probability output. Weight initialization is emphasized, with the importance of non-zero initial weights and the recommendation of Xavier initialization. The optimization process is similar to logistic regression but requires careful weight setting. A practical demonstration shows a three hidden layer neural network learning decision boundaries and minimizing loss, with cross-entropy loss decreasing to 0.45. An interactive module allows users to experiment with neural network architectures, activation functions, learning rates, and batch sizes, highlighting the significance of these parameters in optimizing performance.

children.title: Neural Network Structure and Activation Functions
children.summary: The discussion covers the limitations of logistic regression in predictions and transitions to constructing a neural network with two hidden layers configured as 2x8 and 8x8. The output from these layers is combined into a single value, utilizing a linear layer followed by a tanh non-linearity to produce outputs between -1 and 1. The final output layer employs a sigmoid function to convert this result into a probability ranging from 0 to 1, thereby completing the neural network architecture.

children.title: Neural Network Structure and Activation Functions
children.summary: A logistic regression model is discussed, highlighting its limitations in making predictions. The lecture transitions to constructing a neural network with two hidden layers, specifically a 2x8 and an 8x8 configuration, before combining the outputs into a single value. The process includes applying a linear layer followed by a tanh non-linearity, which outputs values between -1 and 1. The final output layer uses a sigmoid function to convert the result into a probability between 0 and 1, completing the neural network architecture.

So it's a two hidden layers I take two inputs, make an 8, 8 neuron layer, beat an 8 neuron, 8 input to 8 output neuron layer and then combine those results. I apply that hidden layer. That's just a linear layer. Then I put a tan H non-linearity. I could use any number of non-linearities tan H is another one. It's like a sigmoid bit goes from negative 1 to 1. Then I take the output of that, that's the output my first in layer, I call that x again, I make that the input to my next in layer. def forward(self, x):
X = torch.tanh(self.hidden1(x))

p = torch.sigmoid(self.output(x))
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)

How we initialize the weights can have a big impact on how well our model trains. With logistic regression we can set all the weights to zero and be
fine. With a neural network we cannot. Why? However, small starting weights are generally a good idea. Xavier initialization is a popular method for
initializing weights in a neural network. It sets the weights to be normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 2/(number of input units + number of
output units). PyTorch has a function for this in torch. nn. init .

def initialize_weights(model):
for layer in model. children():
if isinstance(layer, nn. Linear) :
nn.init.xavier_uniform_(layer.weight)
nn.init.zeros_(layer.bias)

initializing weights in a neural network. It sets the weights to be normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 2/(number of input units + number of

children.title: Neural Network Weight Initialization and Optimization
children.summary: Weight initialization is critical in neural networks, as setting weights to zero can hinder optimization. Instead, weights should be initialized to small values close to zero, with Xavier initialization being a recommended method that uses a normal distribution with mean 0 and variance based on the number of input and output units. The optimization process for neural networks can utilize the same code as for logistic regression, but unlike logistic regression, neural networks require non-zero initial weights. A practical demonstration illustrates a three hidden layer neural network learning decision boundaries and minimizing loss. The segment also discusses model optimization using cross-entropy loss, which has successfully decreased to 0.45, reflecting improved classification probabilities and includes visualizations of decision boundaries and test loss during training.

children.title: Neural Network Weight Initialization Techniques
children.summary: When building neural networks, it is crucial to initialize weights properly; setting them to zero can prevent optimization. Instead, weights are often initialized to small values close to zero for stable optimization. Xavier initialization is a recommended method, which sets weights to a normal distribution with mean 0 and variance 2 divided by the sum of input and output units. This approach can significantly affect model training efficiency compared to logistic regression, where zero weights are acceptable.

Audio: So I did. So input through a non-linear, through a, a, a, a layer that takes two inputs and produces eight outputs. That's my new output. Now I have eight dimensions, eight hidden outputs. I feed that into something that's eight by eight. So it takes eight things in, produce another eight. And I take those eight and collect them back to one. When we build neural nets, we can't initialize the weights to just zero or one. It won't work. In fact, you could think through it by make all my weight zero what happens to these models. I can no longer optimize them. So we tend to initialize them to something that's close to zero, so it's a stable optimization process, but not truly zero. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So I did. So input through a non-linear, through a, a, a, a layer that takes two inputs and produces eight outputs. That's my new output. Now I have eight dimensions, eight hidden outputs. I feed that into something that's eight by eight. So it takes eight things in, produce another eight. And I take those eight and collect them back to one. When we build neural nets, we can't initialize the weights to just zero or one. It won't work. In fact, you could think through it by make all my weight zero what happens to these models. I can no longer optimize them. So we tend to initialize them to something that's close to zero, so it's a stable optimization process, but not truly zero.

children.title: Neural Network Optimization and Weight Initialization
children.summary: The segment discusses the optimization of neural network models, emphasizing the importance of weight initialization. It highlights that the same optimization code used for logistic regression can be applied to neural networks. A demonstration shows a three hidden layer neural network in real-time as it learns decision boundaries and minimizes loss while fitting a dataset. Additionally, it explains that unlike logistic regression, neural networks cannot have all weights initialized to zero; instead, small starting weights are preferred. Xavier initialization is introduced as a method for setting weights, distributing them normally with mean 0 and variance based on input and output units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There's a whole field of how to better optimize your models or how to better initialize them. I have a model. I can initialize my weights for that model and I can use the same logistic regression or optimization code I use for logistic regression for this neural net. Same code. And now we get to see it happen. This is the cool stuff. So this is now we're running in real time. It's trying to classify this data. These are the decision boundaries you can zoom out a little bit so it's going on. This is decision boundary it's learning to try to classify this. This is the loss as it goes in real time. So you know a little bit. You can see it's getting closer and closer to fitting the data. So this is my three hidden layer neural network optimizing for this data set. And when I am done. which is going slowly. When I'm done, I should have something that is pretty close to fitting my data. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

How we initialize the weights can have a big impact on how well our model trains. With logistic regression we can set all the weights to zero and be
fine. With a neural network we cannot. Why? However, small starting weights are generally a good idea. Xavier initialization is a popular method for
initializing weights in a neural network. It sets the weights to be normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 2/(number of input units + number of
output units). PyTorch has a function for this in torch. nn. init

I can initialize my weights for that model and I can use the same logistic regression or optimization code I use for logistic regression for this neural net. Same code. And now we get to see it happen. This is the cool stuff. So this is now we're running in real time. It's trying to classify this data. These are the decision boundaries you can zoom out a little bit so it's going on. This is decision boundary it's learning to try to classify this. This is the loss as it goes in real time. So you know a little bit. You can see it's getting closer and closer to fitting the data. So this is my three hidden layer neural network optimizing for this data set. And when I am done. which is going slowly. When I'm done, I should have something that is pretty close to fitting my data.

How we initialize the weights can have a big impact on how well our model trains. With logistic regression we can set all the weights to zero and be
fine. With a neural network we cannot. Why? However, small starting weights are generally a good idea. Xavier initialization is a popular method for
initializing weights in a neural network. It sets the weights to be normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 2/(number of input units + number of
output units). PyTorch has a function for this in torch.nn. init

children.title: Model Optimization and Cross-Entropy Loss
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the optimization of a model using cross-entropy loss, which has decreased to 0.45, indicating successful optimization. The transition in Z values from class zero to class one reflects an increase in the probability of being classified as class one. Additionally, there is mention of visualizing decision boundaries and test loss using figures generated during the model training process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You super cool, right? You'll notice that probably transitions are some uncertainty of zoom in here that we can Zoom in here. There's some transition that Z values my probability as I go from class zero, Z is zero. I'll over here to class one, getting higher probability, being class one. You saw my loss. children.title: Neural Network Visualization and Parameter Tuning
children.summary: An interactive module allows experimentation with neural network architectures, enabling the selection of activation functions, number of hidden layers, and neurons per layer. Users can optimize models by adjusting learning rates and batch sizes, and visualize decision boundaries and loss metrics through plots. Understanding the impact of parameters like batch size and learning rate is crucial for optimizing performance, as these adjustments can improve learning curves. Resources such as the PyTorch site provide tutorials for building classifiers, and future lectures will address large language models, including their training and prediction mechanisms.

children.title: Interactive Neural Network Visualization
children.summary: An interactive module allows experimentation with neural network architectures, including selection of activation functions, number of hidden layers, and neurons per layer. Users can optimize models by adjusting learning rates and batch sizes, and visualize decision boundaries and loss metrics through plots.

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

def update_model(n_layers, neurons_per_layer, activation_fn, learning_rate, batch_size, epochs):

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

leaf_chunk.audio: So that is one example of a simple architecture. You can play with this offline. Here's a neural network module, a narysexure. This is a very good one. This module lets you pick different activation functions, different number of hidden layers, number of neurons in each hidden layer. these and see how they fail or succeed. So this is now an interactive visualization. So I can change this as two hidden layers. I can say I want. Let's do three hidden layers. Can run the optimization again. It's pretty good. I can change the learning rate and batch sizes.

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

children.title: Tuning Parameters for Neural Networks
children.summary: Understanding the impact of parameters like batch size and learning rate is crucial for optimizing neural network performance. Adjusting these parameters can smooth out learning curves, leading to better results. Practically, one would download existing models and modify these settings to improve outcomes. Resources like the PyTorch site offer tutorials for building classifiers, and upcoming lectures will cover the workings of large language models, including their training and prediction mechanisms.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You know, we talked about these in class because you need to know them if I set them wrong. But this high maybe I said the batch size low what's gonna happen. It's gonna go all over the place. Can optimize. Right. So these are why we need to know about these parameters for test to cast the gradient. This is how you will set these models. In practice, you will download someone else's model and change these things until you get results that go down and look nice and smooth. If you're looking at a learning curve like this you go, hmm, that is too noisy. Let's make the batch size a little bigger. and it's make the linear a little bit smaller. That's a better learning, better curve. So I'm pretty happy with that result. So this is how you would use neural networks in practice. You'd build these models. You would probably download someone's optimization loop and you'd tune these parameters. As you build more models and you go and learn about PyTorch, you'll learn how to build these individual layers and compose other people's models as building blocks inside of your room. This is stuff you can learn pretty easily if you go to the PyTroTrups site. There are demos in one hour you can build a classifier that does, you know, the digits problem as well as was done in the 1990s and in fact what is used today. So this is the basics of neural networks. If you come to lecture on Thursday, we will talk about how large language models work and I will explain all the details of how we train them and why they're able to make, well, I guess how they make the predictions they make today. So I hope to see you all on Thursday for large language models. 知恶 Thank you. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)

def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)
elif activation == 'tanh':
return torch. tanh (x)

super() .__ init __ ()
self.layers = nn.ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self.layers.append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
I

self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)

leaf_chunk.audio: You know, we talked about these in class because you need to know them if I set them wrong. But this high maybe I said the batch size low what's gonna happen. It's gonna go all over the place. Can optimize. Right. So these are why we need to know about these parameters for test to cast the gradient. This is how you will set these models. In practice, you will download someone else's model and change these things until you get results that go down and look nice and smooth. If you're looking at a learning curve like this you go, hmm, that is too noisy. Let's make the batch size a little bigger. and it's make the linear a little bit smaller. That's a better learning, better curve. So I'm pretty happy with that result. So this is how you would use neural networks in practice. You'd build these models. You would probably download someone's optimization loop and you'd tune these parameters. As you build more models and you go and learn about PyTorch, you'll learn how to build these individual layers and compose other people's models as building blocks inside of your room. This is stuff you can learn pretty easily if you go to the PyTroTrups site. There are demos in one hour you can build a classifier that does, you know, the digits problem as well as was done in the 1990s and in fact what is used today. So this is the basics of neural networks. If you come to lecture on Thursday, we will talk about how large language models work and I will explain all the details of how we train them and why they're able to make, well, I guess how they make the predictions they make today. So I hope to see you all on Thursday for large language models. 知恶 Thank you.
